title: Divine Origins of the Holy Scriptures
speaker: Mr. Steve Thomas
source_files: 03-09-25 - Divine Origins of the Holy Scriptures - Mister Steve Thomas.txt

outline.title: Introduction: What the Bible Claims About Itself
subpoints: The lesson begins with expectations: without the Spirit's work, no argument will convince anyone of divine inspiration — but for the believer, evidence can buttress faith
2 Timothy 3:16-17: theopneustos — God-breathed; 'all Scripture is given by inspiration of God' — the claim itself matters
2 Peter 1:21: 'prophecy never came by the will of man, but holy men of God spoke as they were moved by the Holy Spirit' — the prophets suffered greatly rather than silence the message
Hebrews 4:12: 'the Word of God is living and powerful and sharper than any two-edged sword' — far greater than human
If Scripture claims to be God's Word, that claim must be either true or false — there is no neutral position

outline.title: 1. The Testimony of the Church
subpoints: Westminster Confession 1.5: 'We may be moved and induced by the testimony of the church to a high and reverend esteem of the Holy Scripture'
1 Timothy 3:15: the church is 'the pillar and ground of the truth' — we should care what the capital-C church has said across 2,000 years
Augustine: 'the sacred canon of Scripture... stands so absolutely in a superior position to all later letters of the bishops, that about it we can hold no manner of doubt'
From the church fathers through the Reformers to the present, the Scriptures have held the highest place of authority

outline.title: 2. The Beauty of the Scriptures
subpoints: Heavenliness of the matter: Thomas Watson — 'the mystery of the Scripture is so profound that no man or angel could have known it had it not been revealed; that he who thunders in the heavens should cry in a cradle'
Efficacy of the doctrine: believers know this personally — the Word works in the heart; no other doctrine is so clear and consistent in showing the way to salvation
Majesty of the style: the Scriptures contain history, poetry, wisdom literature, prophecy, apocalyptic, letters, theological treatise — all with beauty and depth
Consent of all the parts: written over approximately 1,500-2,100 years by many authors — yet maintaining a unified message; human consistency over even a decade is difficult, let alone millennia

outline.title: 3. The Scope of the Whole: To Give Glory to God
subpoints: The entire Scripture aims at giving glory to God — not to mankind; fallen man has no motivation to write a document that glorifies something outside himself
The prophets suffered and died for their messages — they had no earthly motivation to do so if they were fabricating
Jeremiah 20:9: the word was like a fire in his bones when he tried to stop prophesying — this is not the behavior of a man inventing a convenient message

outline.title: 4. The Role of the Holy Spirit
subpoints: Westminster Larger Catechism Q4: 'the Spirit of God bearing witness by and with the scriptures in the heart of man is alone able fully to persuade it that they are the very word of God'
1 Corinthians 2:14: 'the natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him' — without regeneration, the evidence is dismissed
This is not esotericism or secret knowledge — it is the Spirit opening eyes to truth available to anyone with the Scriptures

outline.title: 5. Examples: Prophecy and Preservation
subpoints: Prophecy: Isaiah names Cyrus 150 years before he came on the scene (Isaiah 44:28); Psalm 22 and Zechariah describe crucifixion details before crucifixion was invented; Ezekiel predicts the destruction of Tyre in detail

Daniel: historical prophecy so accurate that modern liberal scholars claim it must have been written after the fact — but Ezekiel references Daniel as a contemporary, and Jesus quotes Daniel's prophecies

Manuscript preservation: NT has 5,600 original Greek manuscripts, vs. 643 for Homer's Iliad (the second best-preserved classical text), 49 for Aristotle, 10 for Caesar's Gallic Wars — a qualitative and quantitative difference that points to divine preservation

summary: Mr. Steve Thomas teaches a Sunday school lesson on the divine origins of the Holy Scriptures, part of a series on Scripture. The lesson covers: (1) what the Bible claims about itself — 2 Timothy 3:16-17 (theopneustos, God-breathed), 2 Peter 1:21, Hebrews 4:12 — and why those claims matter for how we view inerrancy; (2) the testimony of the church across 2,000 years (Augustine, the Reformers); (3) the beauty of the Scriptures — heavenliness of the matter (Thomas Watson), efficacy of the doctrine, majesty of style, consent of all parts over 1,500+ years of authorship; (4) the scope of the whole (giving glory to God); (5) the necessity of the Spirit's work; (6) specific examples — prophecy (Cyrus, crucifixion, Tyre, Daniel's historical accuracy) and manuscript preservation (5,600 NT Greek manuscripts vs. 643 for the Iliad).
transcript:
## Introduction: What This Lesson Can and Cannot Do

Before we get into our lesson, a quick review. How many ways can we know there is a God? Three: the light of nature or conscience, the works of God in creation and providence, and the Word and Spirit of God.

For this morning, we want to look at the divine origins of the Holy Scriptures.

Let me set expectations. Without the work of the Spirit, I am not going to convince anyone in this room that the Scriptures are the inspired Word of God. Ultimately, we believe the Scriptures are inspired because of the work of the Spirit in our hearts. I can give you every piece of evidence available, but without the work of the Spirit softening your heart, you will not find it compelling. God could send ten literal plagues, and without the Spirit's work, your heart would remain like Pharaoh's.

For the believer, however, my hope is that this lesson will buttress your faith in the Scriptures as the inspired Word of God.

## What the Bible Claims About Itself

You might wonder: why do we start here rather than just going to the proof? Because what the Bible says about itself matters immensely. We believe the Bible is inerrant and infallible, so what it says about itself has to be true — or we have a serious problem.

If the Bible claimed to be solely written by man, we would need to take that seriously. If it claims to be inspired by God, then that claim must be true — or the Bible contains error and we have no reason to care about the rest of it. Without a strong belief in inerrancy and infallibility, we end up like Thomas Jefferson, who took a razor to the portions of his Bible he found inconvenient. That is where denial of inerrancy leads.

**2 Timothy 3:16-17**: *All Scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work.*

The key word: *theopneustos* — a compound of *theos* (God) and *pneustos* (breathed). God-breathed. Some translations render it 'breathed out by God.' The Scriptures are not a product of human creativity inspired by divine themes — they are God breathing out his Word as a direct reflection of his character.

**2 Peter 1:21**: *Prophecy never came by the will of man, but holy men of God spoke as they were moved by the Holy Spirit.* The prophets were governed by God, not by their own will. Consider Jeremiah: he described the word of God as fire in his bones when he tried to stop prophesying (Jer. 20:9). Consider Ezekiel, whose wife — described as the desire of his eyes — died, and God told him to continue prophesying without stopping to mourn because the prophesying itself was the lesson. These men did not freely choose what to say.

**Hebrews 4:12**: *The word of God is living and powerful and sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing even to the division of soul and spirit, of joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart.* This describes a text far greater than human — one that, as we read it, actually changes us.

Since the Bible unequivocally claims to be God's Word, that claim is either true or false. There is no neutral position.

## The Testimony of the Church

Westminster Confession 1.5 says we may be moved by *the testimony of the church* to a high and reverend esteem of the Scriptures. What does this mean?

The testimony of your local church — including this series — is part of it. But it is more than that: it is 2,000 years of the church saying these Scriptures are important, divinely inspired, the Word of God.

Some might wonder: should we even care what the church says, since we hold to Scripture alone? 1 Timothy 3:15: Paul describes the church as *the pillar and ground of the truth.* We should care what the capital-C church across history has said about Scripture.

Augustine: *The sacred canon of Scripture, both Old and New Testament, is confined within its own limits, and it stands so absolutely in a superior position to all later letters of the bishops, that about it we can hold no manner of doubt or disputation, whether what is confessedly contained in it is right and true.*

From the church fathers, through Augustine, through the Reformers, to the present: the Scriptures have held this highest place. While the Reformers codified the principle of sola scriptura, it is something you can trace through centuries of church history.

## The Beauty of the Scriptures

**Heavenliness of the matter.** Thomas Watson: *The mystery of the Scripture is so profound that no man or angel could have known it had it not been revealed. That he who thunders in the heavens should cry in a cradle, that the Prince of Life should die, that sin should be punished to the full, yet pardon to the full — who could have conceived of such a mystery had not the Scriptures revealed it to us?* What we find in the Scriptures is so far above what we would have come up with on our own.

**Efficacy of the doctrine.** No other doctrine is so clear and consistent in showing the way of salvation. For believers, this is experiential: you have felt the Word work in your heart, you have felt the call of the Spirit, you know this doctrine is effectual.

**Majesty of the style and consent of all the parts.** The Scriptures contain history, poetry, wisdom literature, prophecy, apocalyptic writing, letters, theological treatises — all with beauty and depth. But more than that: they were written over approximately 1,500 to 2,100 years by many different authors — and they maintain a unified message throughout.

Think about how much changes in your workplace over a decade. Think about how much the positions of our nation have shifted in 250 years. Humans struggle to hold true to anything over even a few years. How much more extraordinary for authors across two millennia to produce a unified message? This is not possible without divine inspiration.

## The Scope of the Whole

The entire scope of Scripture is to give glory to God. Fallen man has no motivation to write a document that glorifies something outside himself, that calls out the sins we love most, that requires giving up our own self-sufficiency. The very content of Scripture argues against it being the product of self-interested human authorship.

## The Role of the Holy Spirit

Despite all the beauty we have just described, without the inward work of the Spirit, our eyes remain closed. 1 Corinthians 2:14: *The natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him; nor can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned.*

This is not esotericism — we are not talking about secret knowledge gained through spiritual experiences. We are talking about the Spirit opening our eyes to truth that is available to anyone with a copy of the Scriptures. Without regeneration, that truth is dismissed as foolishness. With the Spirit's work, it is recognizable as exactly what it claims to be.

## Examples: Prophecy and Preservation

**Prophecy.** Deuteronomy gives Israel a test for true prophecy: does the prophet speak in harmony with God's revealed Word, and do short-term predictions come true?

Isaiah names Cyrus by name approximately 150 years before Cyrus came on the scene — before there was any king named Cyrus to predict. Psalm 22 and other Old Testament passages describe the details of crucifixion — pierced hands and feet, garments divided by lot, none of his bones broken — before crucifixion had even been invented as a form of execution. Ezekiel predicted the destruction of Tyre in detail, including that the surface of the rock would be scraped and the city would become a place for drying fishing nets. All fulfilled.

The book of Daniel contains historical prophecy so accurate that modern liberal scholars have argued it must have been written after the events it describes — because there is no way it could have been that accurate. But Daniel was referenced by Ezekiel as a contemporary, and Jesus quoted Daniel's prophecies as legitimate. To the unbeliever, Daniel's accuracy is a stumbling block. To us, it is one of the strongest examples of divine inspiration — God pronouncing the succession of world kingdoms centuries before they occurred.

**Manuscript preservation.** A quick comparison: Julius Caesar's *Gallic Wars* — 10 surviving manuscripts. Aristotle's works — 49. Homer's *Iliad* (widely considered the second best-preserved classical text) — 643. The New Testament in original Greek — approximately 5,600 manuscripts. A full order of magnitude beyond the Iliad.

Additionally, the vast majority of the New Testament is quoted in the writings of the early church fathers from the second and third centuries — meaning we could reconstruct almost the entire New Testament without any New Testament manuscripts at all. This level of preservation is unique to the Bible. It is not something that happens to books written by men. It points to a preservation that is itself divine.

## Key Takeaways

First: the Bible unequivocally claims to be the inspired Word of God, and that claim has to impact how we view it. Second: that claim is bolstered by the content, beauty, efficacy, and preservation of the Bible. Third: if you want to know God's will, it is revealed in your Bible. If you want to hear God speak, read your Bible.

Father, thank you for this book that you have given us — your Word that is inspired and breathed out by you. Drive us to love it, study it, and let it impact our lives. Thank you that we live in a time when we have easy access to it. In Jesus' name, amen.
